chunk_summary: This section discusses the endocrine role of adipose tissue, puberty-related insulin resistance, and relapse physiology in pediatric obesity, with a focus on India-specific risks and clinical strategies for management.
learning_objectives: Understand the endocrine functions of adipose tissue and its role in insulin resistance and metabolic dysregulation.
Recognize the impact of puberty on insulin sensitivity and its implications for screening in adolescents with obesity.
Identify the role of sleep and circadian rhythms in appetite regulation and obesity management.
Learn strategies to address weight loss plateaus and prevent relapse through maintenance planning.
key_facts: Adipose tissue acts as an endocrine organ, producing adipokines and inflammatory mediators that influence insulin sensitivity and appetite regulation.
Insulin sensitivity normally decreases during puberty, requiring proactive screening for dysglycemia and PCOS in high-risk children around age 10.
Short sleep and irregular circadian rhythms disrupt appetite regulation and insulin sensitivity, increasing late-night eating and reducing adherence to activity plans.
Elevated ALT levels in children with obesity should be treated as a systemic risk marker, not just a liver-specific issue.
Adaptive thermogenesis after weight loss can lead to increased appetite and reduced energy expenditure, necessitating explicit maintenance strategies.
reference_quote: "Adipose tissue functions as an endocrine organ, producing adipokines and inflammatory mediators that influence insulin sensitivity, appetite signaling, and ectopic fat deposition." (Source: Adipose Tissue as a Target for Precision Medicine in Metabolic Disease, Diabetes)
document_key_topics: Adipose endocrinology and its role in obesity
Pubertal insulin resistance and screening strategies
Impact of sleep and circadian rhythms on obesity
Relapse physiology and maintenance planning in weight management
